title: Decomposition & Abstraction
subtitle: Break It Down, Keep It Simple!
overview: This session introduces students to two powerful computational thinking skills — Decomposition (breaking a big problem into smaller parts) and Abstraction (hiding unnecessary details and focusing on what matters). Using fun real-life examples like making a pizza, planning a school trip, and building a Lego set, students will learn how programmers tackle complex problems step by step.

images._note: Add photos from the actual session — whiteboard breakdowns, student group activity sheets, abstraction sorting games, puzzle activities.

keyTopics: What is Decomposition? — Breaking a big problem into smaller, manageable parts
Real-life Decomposition — Making pizza, planning a trip, building a Lego set
Why Decomposition Helps — Easier to solve, test, and fix smaller parts
What is Abstraction? — Hiding unnecessary details, focusing on what matters
Real-life Abstraction — Google Maps, car dashboard, TV remote
How Programmers Use Both Skills Together

resources.title: Scratch — Build by Breaking Into Parts

activity.title: Pizza Problem Decomposition + Abstraction Card Sort
steps: Teacher writes 'Plan a School Trip' on the board as the big problem
Groups brainstorm and list ALL the smaller tasks needed on sticky notes
Groups arrange sticky notes into categories (Transport, Food, Permission, Activities)
Discuss: How did breaking it down make it easier to solve?
Now play the Abstraction Card Sort — each group gets cards with details
Students sort cards: IMPORTANT (keep) vs NOT IMPORTANT (hide/ignore)
Groups share their sorting and justify their choices
Teacher connects both activities: Decomposition = break it down, Abstraction = focus on what matters
Students write a short pseudocode plan using both skills for a chosen problem

quiz.q: What is Decomposition in computational thinking?
options: Breaking a big problem into smaller, easier parts
quiz.explanation: Decomposition means splitting a complex problem into smaller pieces that are easier to understand and solve.

quiz.q: Which of these is the best example of Decomposition?
options: Planning a birthday party by listing: food, decorations, invites, games
quiz.explanation: Breaking a party into sub-tasks (food, decorations, invites, games) is decomposition — managing each piece separately!

options: To solve smaller parts one at a time, making work easier
quiz.explanation: Decomposition makes big problems manageable. Fix one small part at a time — less confusion, fewer errors!

options: Adding more steps to a solution
Focusing on important details and hiding unnecessary ones
quiz.explanation: Abstraction = filter! You keep what matters and ignore what doesn't, to keep things simple and clear.

quiz.q: Which of these is an example of Abstraction?
options: Learning every mechanical part of a car engine to drive a car
Using a TV remote without knowing how it works inside
Writing all steps from scratch every time
Memorizing every street in a city
quiz.explanation: You use a TV remote without understanding its circuits — that's abstraction! You only need to know what buttons do.

quiz.q: Google Maps shows roads and landmarks but hides underground pipes and wiring. This is an example of:
quiz.explanation: Google Maps hides irrelevant details (pipes, wires) and shows only what's useful for navigation. That's abstraction!

quiz.q: A student wants to build a school website. She splits it into: Home Page, About Page, Contact Page, and Gallery. She is using:
quiz.explanation: Breaking the website into separate page tasks is decomposition — solve one page at a time!

quiz.q: What is the main DIFFERENCE between Decomposition and Abstraction?
options: They are exactly the same thing
Decomposition breaks problems into parts; Abstraction filters out unnecessary details
Abstraction breaks problems into parts; Decomposition filters details
Both are only used in mathematics
quiz.explanation: Decomposition = Break It Down. Abstraction = Keep What Matters. Two different but related skills!

quiz.q: Making a pizza can be decomposed into: prepare dough, add sauce, add toppings, bake. What happens if you SKIP decomposition?
options: Steps get confused, things go wrong, problem becomes harder
quiz.explanation: Without decomposition, you might forget steps, do them in the wrong order, or get overwhelmed. Chaos!

quiz.q: A car dashboard shows speed and fuel level but hides engine details. This is:
quiz.explanation: The dashboard abstracts engine complexity — you see only what you need to drive safely. Classic abstraction!

quiz.q: Lego building instructions break the final model into small step-by-step stages. This is an example of:
quiz.explanation: Each Lego step is a small sub-task. Together they build the full model — that's decomposition in action!

quiz.q: Which skill helps a programmer focus on WHAT a function does without worrying about HOW it works inside?
quiz.explanation: Abstraction lets you use functions/tools without knowing every internal detail. Focus on WHAT, not HOW.

quiz.q: A recipe can be seen as a real-life example of:
quiz.explanation: A recipe decomposes cooking into smaller steps AND is an algorithm — ordered instructions to reach a goal!

quiz.q: When solving any big problem, what is the CORRECT order?
options: Decompose the problem → Identify key details (Abstraction) → Plan algorithm → Code
quiz.explanation: Good programmers decompose first, then abstract key details, then plan an algorithm, then write code. Always plan!

quiz.q: Which two skills are part of the four pillars of Computational Thinking covered in this session?
quiz.explanation: Decomposition and Abstraction are two of the four pillars of Computational Thinking — essential skills for every programmer!
